docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: TerroirOS documentation is canonical in this repository. New documentation changes should be proposed here first.

docs.title: Principles
docs.text:
• Keep wording accurate to current implementation status.
  • Preserve evidence-backed claims with verifiable sources.
  • Maintain bilingual parity where applicable (English and Georgian).

docs.title: Local workflow
docs.text:
1. Install Node.js 20+ and Python 3.11+.
  2. Install Node dependencies: npm install.
  3. Install Python dependencies: pip install -r requirements.txt.
  4. Run sync from website source if needed: npm run docs:sync -- /absolute/path/to/TerroirOS-Website.
  5. Run validation: npm run docs:check and mkdocs build --strict.

docs.title: Contribution process
docs.text:
The contribution process should help people improve the project without accidentally widening the gap between documentation and implementation reality.

Current process guidance

This page reflects how contributors should approach changes in the current repository, especially for trust-sensitive documentation.

docs.title: Rules that matter most
docs.text:
• Do not let the docs imply live functionality that the repo does not contain.

  • Prefer primary or authoritative sources when adding externally grounded claims.

  • Use plain-language summaries before technical depth so the docs serve mixed audiences.

  • Preserve bilingual stability for the translated pages that already exist.

docs.title: Documentation contribution flow
docs.text:
1. Confirm the current repo state - Inspect the code before editing docs that describe implementation.

  2. Gather sources - Use authoritative sources and add inline citations when the page makes factual external claims.

  3. Update content and navigation together - When a page moves or expands, keep the sidebar and related pages aligned.

  4. Lint and review trust language - Treat wording around verification, guarantees, and implementation status as a review focus, not just grammar.

docs.title: FAQ
docs.text:
The FAQ answers the questions people tend to ask when they are still deciding whether Terroir is a website, a concept, a future product, or an open infrastructure effort.

Public explainer

This page is part of the current documentation experience and intentionally mixes plain-language answers with candid implementation context.

docs.title: Is Terroir already a full working provenance platform?
docs.text: Not in this repository. What exists today is the public website, documentation, research library, and content framing for the broader platform direction.

docs.title: Why does the documentation still describe future APIs and contracts?
docs.text: Because those interfaces are still useful as reference architecture and planning material, but the rewritten docs now mark them clearly as planned or reference-only.

docs.title: Why start with Georgian wine?
docs.text: Because origin is central to its value, the cultural context is unusually strong, and the use case makes the trust problem easy to understand for both technical and non-technical readers.

docs.title: Can these ideas apply beyond wine?
docs.text: Yes. The goal is a reusable origin-and-attestation pattern that can later support other crops and regions without treating Georgian wine as the only valid use case.

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text:
The glossary defines the terms that appear repeatedly across the docs so readers from different backgrounds can keep a shared mental model.

Current reference aid

This glossary supports the current documentation set and will likely expand as implementation work adds more concrete interfaces.

docs.title: Key terms
docs.text:
• Attestation: A structured claim made by a specific actor about a batch, process, or event.

  • Batch / passport: The stable record describing a concrete product lot and the trust story that accumulates around it.

  • Integrity commitment: A hash or similar commitment that helps later readers detect silent change.

  • Issuer: An actor whose role allows them to make a certain class of attestation.

  • Verification view: The readable surface that packages the underlying provenance record for review by another person.

  • Superseded: A state indicating that a record or claim has been replaced by a newer authoritative version rather than silently edited.

docs.title: Governance roadmap
docs.text:
The governance roadmap explains how Terroir could move from a team-led public project into a more durable community-governed trust infrastructure.

Public roadmap

This page documents intended governance direction alongside the current documentation and website implementation.

docs.title: Why governance matters here
docs.text:
Provenance infrastructure only becomes trustworthy if the rules around issuers, schemas, and verification do not quietly collapse into private control. Governance is therefore part of the product, not a later administrative extra.

The digital public goods lens is useful here because it treats openness, documentation, and community accountability as part of long-term infrastructure quality rather than just branding.[1]

docs.title: Governance progression
docs.text: Phase Primary focus What success looks like Foundation Clear docs, transparent architecture, and a coherent pilot story. People can understand the system boundary and contribute without guesswork. Operational pilots Limited live workflows with explicit governance roles. A small set of trusted actors can run the system without hiding decisions. Shared stewardship Broader schema and issuer governance with documented review paths. Changes become discussable and reversible by the community, not just a single team.

docs.title: Plain-language explanation
docs.text: An open-source license answers a practical question: what can another person or organization legally do with the code? Terroir documentation should make that clear up front because trust infrastructure loses credibility if reuse rights are vague.

docs.title: Architecture overview
docs.text:
The current repository ships the documentation and marketing layer, while the docs also describe a broader reference architecture for future provenance workflows, data storage, attestations, and verification.

Implemented website + reference architecture

This page deliberately separates what the website already implements from the broader system Terroir intends to support later.

docs.title: The most important distinction to keep in mind
docs.text:
Layer Present in this repository today Described as planned/reference Website Marketing pages, whitepaper, research library, docs navigation, and docs content. A richer documentation index and future trust-facing onboarding surfaces. Demo A demo gate page that stores an email locally before redirecting. A real producer/certifier workflow, verification surfaces, and working portal routes. Application services Not present. Batch management, attestations, public verification endpoints, and role-aware workflows. Data/storage Not present. Structured provenance records and evidence storage. Integrity layer Not present. Selective anchoring and role-controlled attestation commitments.

Why this distinction matters

The earlier docs blurred the line between public concept and shipped code. This rewrite keeps the vision intact while making the current repository boundary explicit.

docs.title: On-chain/off-chain split in the reference model
docs.text:
The planned system keeps human-readable evidence off-chain while using a narrower integrity layer for critical commitments. 1 2 Alt text: A split diagram with an application side containing docs, evidence, workflows, and verification views, and an integrity side containing hashes, roles, and tamper-evident commitments. Layout: split - Application and evidence layer - Product passport data: Human-readable records for producers, batches, and supporting evidence. - Verification pages: Readable pages, export views, and QR-linked experiences for non-technical users. - Workflow logic: Authorization, review states, and submission rules. - Integrity and authorization layer - Content commitments: Hashes or attestations that make later tampering visible. - Role control: Rules about who can write which commitments. - Public audit trail: Independent checks without exposing all underlying private data.

That split is deliberately conservative. It follows a pattern seen in modern attestation systems: keep the human workflow understandable and flexible, and reserve the integrity layer for the part that benefits from stronger independent verification.[1][2]

docs.title: Core concepts
docs.text:
Terroir revolves around a handful of reusable concepts: producers, batches, attestations, verification views, and the trust rules that connect them.

Conceptual model

These concepts describe the reference model used throughout the docs. They are not yet represented as a full application model inside this website repository.

docs.title: The five ideas to keep in mind
docs.text:
A producer is the organization or person making the origin claim. A batch is the concrete unit of product that the claim refers to, such as one harvest lot, shipment lot, or packaged production run.

An attestation is a structured statement made by another actor about that batch, such as a certifier, lab, cooperative, logistics partner, or buyer. A verification view is the readable surface that assembles the underlying record into something another person can inspect.

Behind those objects sits a trust rule: not every actor can say everything. Good provenance design limits who can issue which kinds of statements and how those statements are checked later.[1][2]

docs.title: How the core concepts fit together
docs.text: A batch sits at the center, with supporting claims and verification evidence attached around it. Alt text: An anatomy diagram with Batch passport in the center and surrounding nodes for producer, certifier, evidence, verification view, and integrity anchor. Layout: anatomy - Center: Batch passport - Producer: Defines the product, place, and production context. - Attestor: Adds an accountable claim about quality, origin, process, or movement. - Evidence: Photos, inspections, documents, or lab results linked to the claim. - Integrity layer: Makes important changes visible instead of silent. - Verification view: Turns the record into something a human can read and challenge.

docs.title: What is Terroir
docs.text:
Terroir is a public-facing project and documentation site for an open approach to trusted agricultural origin, starting with Georgian wine and designed to grow into a reusable provenance stack.

Project overview

This repository currently contains the Terroir website and documentation experience. The broader provenance platform described here is the project direction and reference model.

docs.title: In plain language
docs.text:
Terroir is an attempt to make product origin easier to trust. The idea is simple: if a producer says where a product came from, who handled it, and what was verified about it, other people should be able to inspect that claim without relying on opaque paperwork or a single private platform.

What exists today

The code in this repository delivers the marketing site, whitepaper, research library, bilingual documentation shell, and a demo gate. It does not yet ship the full producer, certifier, database, smart-contract, or API implementation described in several reference pages.

The project starts in Georgian wine because origin is unusually important there: geography, cultivation method, and local heritage are part of the product's meaning as well as its market value.[1][2]

docs.title: Why the project exists
docs.text:
High-value agricultural goods often depend on claims that are hard to verify quickly: where the crop was grown, whether a certifier inspected it, whether the shipment stayed linked to the right lot, or whether a label legitimately represents a protected origin. Terroir proposes an open way to organize those claims so they can be checked by more than one party.

The project is also shaped by a digital public goods mindset. Instead of turning provenance into another closed vendor dependency, the long-term goal is to publish reusable schemas, verification patterns, and reference interfaces that communities can adapt to their own crops, regions, and regulatory environments.[3]

docs.title: How the trust story is supposed to work
Provenance lifecycle
docs.text:
Terroir treats provenance as a sequence of accountable handoffs, not a single branding claim. Alt text: A six-step flow showing land and harvest, batch creation, attestations, verification packaging, public checks, and feedback into future trust decisions. Layout: flow - Origin captured: A producer records where and how a batch began. - Batch defined: The product is described as a concrete lot or passport. - Claims attested: Inspectors, labs, or certifiers add signed evidence. - Integrity anchored: Important records are committed to a tamper-evident reference layer. - Verification shared: Buyers, regulators, and consumers get a readable proof path. - Trust reused: The same record supports export, compliance, and storytelling.

This pattern is intentionally broader than a single technology choice. A database, an attestation protocol, QR-linked verification pages, and selective ledger anchoring can all play a role. The design question is not 'how much blockchain can we add?' but 'which evidence needs to stay independently checkable over time?'

docs.title: Why trusted origin matters
docs.text:
Origin claims affect market access, fraud risk, public trust, and cultural value, which is why provenance needs to be understandable to everyday users as well as auditors and developers.

Research-backed explainer

This page synthesizes public research and sector guidance to explain the problem that Terroir is trying to address.

docs.title: The problem behind the promise
docs.text:
Traceability is not valuable because it sounds modern. It matters because producers, importers, regulators, and consumers frequently have to make decisions with incomplete or non-verifiable evidence. Once a label is easy to imitate and hard to audit, the most careful producers lose the ability to distinguish themselves.

Official guidance from the food-systems world treats traceability as a practical capability: it supports recalls, accountability, and chain visibility.[1][2] In high-value categories, anti-counterfeit work makes the same point from a different angle: when fraudulent goods circulate globally, trustworthy origin evidence becomes part of risk management rather than just marketing.[3]

docs.title: Why origin claims deserve special care
Why the same data matters to different people
docs.text:
Stakeholder Why origin matters What goes wrong without proof Producer Origin supports price, reputation, and market differentiation. Authentic work becomes indistinguishable from imitation. Certifier Origin is often tied to inspection scope and compliance rules. Paper or siloed records slow verification and weaken enforcement. Importer or buyer Origin affects purchasing risk and downstream customer trust. Disputes increase and due-diligence costs stay high. Everyday consumer Origin helps people connect price and story to evidence. The buyer has to trust branding alone.

For Georgian wine in particular, origin is not just a shipping fact. It carries archaeological, regional, and cultural significance, including the qvevri winemaking tradition highlighted by UNESCO.[4]

docs.title: What trustworthy origin should feel like
Trust design principles
docs.text:
Terroir documentation uses these principles to explain the platform concept without assuming a specialized technical audience.

  1. Readable first - A buyer or consumer should understand the claim before they need technical context.

  2. Evidence linked - Important claims should point to who made them and what record supports them.

  3. Tampering visible - If key records change later, that should be detectable instead of invisible.

  4. Portable across actors - The record should survive handoffs between producers, certifiers, exporters, and buyers.

docs.title: Attestation endpoints
docs.text:
Future attestation endpoints should help issuers create accountable, role-scoped claims without turning the API into an unreviewable free-form note system.

Reference issuer endpoint

This page documents a future interface shape only. There is no live attestation API in the current repository.

docs.title: How to read this page
docs.text:
Attestation endpoints should help the right issuers make the right kinds of claims, in a shape that later verification can still explain clearly.

Current repo reality

No attestation service implementation is present in this repository.

docs.title: Attestation endpoint expectations
docs.text: Concern Expected behavior Why it matters Issuer scope Check whether the actor can issue the requested claim type. Authority matters as much as authentication. Structured payloads Use claim-type-aware fields instead of arbitrary notes. Readable verification depends on consistent shapes. Evidence references Link supporting material to the claim. Claims without evidence are hard to trust or challenge. Status history Allow superseded or challenged states instead of destructive edits. Trust requires visible history.

docs.title: Design and delivery guidance
docs.text:
• Keep claim classes explicit and documented.

  • Preserve issuer identity and scope in the response model.

  • Favor append-and-supersede patterns over hard overwrite for trust-critical changes.

docs.title: API authentication
docs.text:
The future API should authenticate different actors clearly and distinguish public verification from privileged write actions.

Reference API boundary

No API routes exist in this repository today. This page documents the trust boundary a future API should preserve.

docs.title: How to read this page
docs.text:
The key rule is simple: public verification should stay easy to read, while actions that create or modify trust-critical records must be strongly tied to actor identity and role.

Current repo reality

There is no app/api implementation in this repository at the moment.

docs.title: Authentication boundary
docs.text: Actor type Authentication expectation Allowed actions Public verifier No account required for reading public trust views. Read verification results only. Producer Authenticated identity linked to organization context. Create or manage producer-side records. Certifier / issuer Authenticated identity plus issuer authority checks. Issue or update attestation records within scope. Service or admin actor Strongly restricted service credentials. Background processing and governance-only actions.

docs.title: Design and delivery guidance
docs.text:
• Separate read-only verification from write-capable service boundaries.

  • Make issuer scope visible instead of encoding it only in backend logic.

  • Avoid leaking privileged credentials into client-side surfaces.

docs.title: Batch creation API
docs.text:
A future batch-creation API should turn producer input into a stable batch passport without confusing draft authoring, publish states, or trust commitments.

Reference producer endpoint

This page documents a future interface shape only. There is no batch-creation endpoint in this repository.

docs.title: How to read this page
docs.text:
Creating a batch should be more than posting a blob of fields. The API needs to establish a stable identity, preserve provenance context, and prepare the record for later attestations.

Current repo reality

The current codebase contains no batch-management backend.

docs.title: Batch-creation responsibilities
docs.text: Concern What the endpoint should do What it should avoid Identity Create a stable batch or passport reference. Changing identifiers every time a detail changes. Validation Check required origin and product fields. Accepting ambiguous or meaningless records. Workflow state Support draft versus publish-ready states. Treating every save as immediately public or final. Commitment timing Commit only when the workflow is ready. Locking noisy draft changes into the integrity layer.

docs.title: Design and delivery guidance
docs.text:
• Keep draft authoring separate from integrity commitment where possible.

  • Return a record shape that downstream attestations can reference cleanly.

  • Make later supersession and correction strategies explicit early.

docs.title: Public verification API
docs.text:
The public verification API should expose clear, graded outcomes without requiring privileged credentials or hiding uncertainty behind a binary pass/fail.

Reference public endpoint

This page documents a future public verification interface only. There is no live /api/verify route in this repository.

docs.title: How to read this page
docs.text:
A public verification interface should be easy to consume, but it should still explain whether a record is verified, incomplete, changed later, or otherwise limited.

Current repo reality

The repository ships content describing verification, not a live verification API or scanner service.

docs.title: Public verification response shape
docs.text: Response element Purpose Trust value Readable summary Lets humans understand the record quickly. Builds trust through clarity. Graded verification state Avoids misleading binary answers. Supports better risk decisions. Attestation chain Shows who made which claims. Supports accountability and challenge. Integrity comparison result Explains whether the current record still matches its commitment. Makes silent change visible.

docs.title: Design and delivery guidance
docs.text:
• Make the API safe for unauthenticated read access where public trust views are intended.

  • Expose enough context for downstream systems to render a readable explanation.

  • Treat incomplete and changed states as first-class response outcomes.

docs.title: Environment variables
docs.text:
The environment variable surface for a future Terroir application should make trust boundaries explicit instead of hiding critical secrets or role assumptions.

Reference configuration surface

docs.title: How to read this page

Environment variables should tell you which services the system depends on and which secrets must never reach the browser.

Current repo reality

The current website repository does not include a live backend service requiring the broader environment described here.

docs.title: Configuration classes
docs.text: Configuration class Examples Handling rule Public app config Base URLs, public locale behavior, non-secret feature flags. Safe for client exposure when truly public. Restricted service config Database URLs, signing keys, issuer credentials. Server-only and tightly controlled. Deployment metadata Environment names, rollout markers, logging endpoints. Visible to operators, not necessarily public.

docs.title: Design and delivery guidance
docs.text:
• Name secrets so their sensitivity is obvious.

  • Separate client-safe and server-only configuration paths early.

  • Document what happens when configuration is absent or inconsistent.

docs.title: Reference frontend architecture
docs.text:
The future operational frontend should help multiple audiences understand trust states clearly, while this repository today focuses on the public website and docs experience.

Reference frontend design

The operational frontend described here is not yet present. The implemented frontend in this repository is the public website and docs.

docs.title: How to read this page
docs.text:
A Terroir frontend should not merely display data. It should help each user understand what they can do, what has been verified, and what still requires attention.

Current repo reality

Today the repo contains the marketing site, research pages, whitepaper, and documentation UI.

docs.title: Frontend surfaces to keep separate
docs.text: Surface Primary job Trust concern Producer workspace Create and maintain records. Avoid accidental publication or commitment of draft data. Issuer workspace Review and issue claims. Make issuer scope and evidence requirements obvious. Public verification page Explain trust state to non-technical readers. Use careful language and graded statuses. Documentation layer Explain the system and its limits. Stay aligned with actual implementation status.

docs.title: Design and delivery guidance
docs.text:
• Design status language first, then visual polish.

  • Preserve consistent trust cues across producer, issuer, and public views.

  • Use the docs site as the truth-telling layer when implementation is still catching up.

Reference data layer

docs.title: Reference data-layer responsibilities
docs.text: Data area Purpose Key design caution Producer and organization records Represent origin actors and issuer identities. Do not mix public profile copy with private governance metadata. Batch records Store stable passport data. Avoid letting important fields mutate silently. Attestation records Store issuer-linked claims. Preserve history and evidence references. Verification outputs Cache or package readable trust views. Do not let cached summaries diverge from source truth without detection.

docs.title: Design and delivery guidance
docs.text:

  • Separate public verification output from restricted operational state.

  • Design the data layer around explanation as well as storage.

docs.title: Contract events
docs.text:
Contract events should make integrity changes auditable and indexable without forcing every downstream tool to poll raw contract state.

Reference event surface

This page describes event patterns for a future contract or integrity layer. No event-producing contract is present here today.

docs.title: How to read this page
docs.text:
Events are the history feed for important trust changes. They help downstream systems notice when a batch was committed, a claim was added, or an authority changed.

Current repo reality

The repository contains documentation only; there is no deployed contract event stream to subscribe to.

docs.title: Event categories worth preserving
docs.text: Event type Why it matters Consumers Record committed Lets systems know a batch or attestation entered the integrity layer. Verification services, auditors, dashboards Issuer authority changed Makes trust-rule changes visible. Governance tooling, risk review Record superseded or challenged Prevents stale trust signals. Buyers, public verification views

docs.title: Design and delivery guidance
docs.text:
• Use event names that map cleanly to human meaning, not just low-level storage actions.

  • Include stable identifiers so off-chain services can reconstruct the timeline accurately.

  • Treat supersession and challenge events as first-class trust events, not edge cases.

docs.title: Deployment guide
docs.text:
This guide explains what future Terroir deployments should separate into environments, governance steps, and trust controls before any integrity-sensitive service goes live.

Reference deployment guide

There is no deployable provenance stack in this repository. The guidance below exists to steer future pilot planning.

docs.title: How to read this page
docs.text:
Deployment for a trust system is not just pushing code. It includes deciding who runs which services, how issuers are approved, how changes are reviewed, and what a rollback means for public verification.

Current repo reality

Today this repository deploys only the public website experience.

docs.title: Deployment questions that matter
docs.text: Deployment concern Questions to answer before go-live Why it matters Environment separation Which services differ between demo, pilot, and production? Avoids mixing public experimentation with trusted records. Issuer governance Who approves and revokes trusted actors? Bad governance weakens the entire trust model. Verification continuity How will old links or QR codes remain readable after updates? Trust records lose value if they rot quickly. Incident handling What happens if data, keys, or claims need urgent review? Trust systems need explicit operational playbooks.

docs.title: Design and delivery guidance
docs.text:
• Treat pilot rollout as both a product and governance exercise.

  • Preserve stable verification URLs wherever possible.

  • Write human-readable incident language before the first incident arrives.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:

  • security-and-limitations - Review the risk model that should shape deployment.
  • governance-roadmap - Pair technical rollout with governance evolution.

docs.title: Function reference
docs.text:
This page describes the kinds of read and write operations a future integrity layer would need to expose in order to support provenance commitments and verification.

Reference interface

The interface below is descriptive guidance only. It is not backed by code in this repository.

docs.title: How to read this page
docs.text:
A good trust interface exposes only a few critical operations: commit a record, read the last commitment, check authority, and surface supersession or dispute states.

Current repo reality

There is no contract ABI or service interface implementation in the current repository.

docs.title: Core interface responsibilities
docs.text: Operation Purpose Notes Commit record Store the integrity commitment for a batch or attestation. Should be role-restricted. Read commitment Return the current commitment for verification. Must be public and stable. Check issuer authority Confirm whether an actor can issue a given class of statement. Needs revocation handling. Mark superseded or disputed Keep trust history readable over time. Should not rely on silent overwrites.

docs.title: Design and delivery guidance
docs.text:
• Optimize for clarity and auditability rather than feature count.

  • Keep read paths easy for public verifiers and downstream services to consume.

  • Model change history explicitly so later readers can see why trust status shifted.

docs.title: Registry contract
docs.text:
The registry contract page documents the intended role of a minimal integrity layer that stores commitments and authorization rules without becoming the entire application.

Reference contract design

No registry contract code exists in this website repository. This page describes the intended responsibilities of that future component.

docs.title: How to read this page
docs.text:
If Terroir later uses an on-chain or similarly tamper-evident registry, that layer should stay narrow. Its job is to hold commitments and role rules, not to store every piece of business logic or user-facing content.

Current repo reality

There is no packages/contracts directory or deployed contract artifact in this repository today.

docs.title: Keep the registry narrow
docs.text: Responsibility Should live in the registry Should stay outside Identity of committed records Yes Human-readable passport views Role-based write permissions Yes Full operational workflows and evidence handling Integrity commitments Yes Large media files or rich document content Search and reporting UX No Application and public website layers

docs.title: Design and delivery guidance
docs.text:
• Prefer minimal state that helps later verification without leaking unnecessary operational detail.

  • Pair registry roles with human governance rather than treating role assignment as purely technical.

  • Design for revocation, supersession, and visible dispute states.

docs.title: Attestation schema
docs.text:
Attestations are the structured claims that give a batch record depth. They explain who said what, in what role, with what evidence, and under which conditions.

Reference attestation model

This schema is documented as the intended shape of future attestations. It is not yet backed by live storage or API code in this repository.

docs.title: Reference fields
docs.text: Field Why it exists Example Attestation ID Lets the claim be referenced independently of the batch. origin-claim-2026-001 Batch reference Connects the claim to the right lot or passport. batch-kakheti-2026-03 Issuer identity Makes authorship reviewable. Regional certifier, lab, cooperative Claim type Keeps different claim classes understandable. origin_verified, organic_reviewed, lab_result Evidence links Shows what supports the claim. inspection report, photo set, lab file Status Tracks whether the claim is active, superseded, challenged, or withdrawn. active

docs.title: Attestation lifecycle
docs.text: A good attestation remains readable even when it later changes state. 1 Alt text: A flow from attestation draft to issued claim, evidence linked, integrity commitment, public verification, and later superseded or challenged states. Layout: flow - Draft: Issuer prepares the claim and supporting evidence. - Issued: The attestation is signed or otherwise published as authoritative. - Evidence linked: Supporting files or documents are attached by reference. - Committed: Critical fields are recorded in an integrity layer. - Reviewed later: The claim can become verified, superseded, challenged, or withdrawn.

docs.title: Batch schema
docs.text:
A batch schema is the smallest trustworthy description of a product lot: enough to identify what is being discussed, where it came from, and which later claims should attach to it.

Reference batch shape

The schema below is a documentation reference target, not a live contract or database object in this repository.

docs.title: Fields worth protecting first
docs.text: Field Why it matters Notes Batch identifier Lets every later claim point to the same product unit. Should be stable and human-referenceable. Producer reference Connects the batch to its origin actor. Can point to an organization record rather than duplicate text. Product type Prevents ambiguous descriptions such as 'agricultural product'. Use a controlled vocabulary where possible. Origin details Captures the region, site, or protected-origin context. Precision depends on privacy and commercial constraints. Time window Anchors the batch in harvest, production, or packaging time. The relevant date can vary by commodity. Current status Shows whether the batch is draft, attested, shipped, superseded, or disputed. Important for user-facing trust signals.

docs.title: Design guidance
docs.text:
The batch schema should stay intentionally lean. Terroir should not attempt to encode every agronomic nuance directly into the core object. The core schema should identify the batch cleanly, while richer context can live in evidence, attestations, or commodity-specific extensions.

Why restraint matters

Overloaded schemas become hard to validate, hard to explain, and hard to carry across commodities. A trustworthy passport starts with a small set of stable fields and expands through linked evidence.

docs.text:

docs.title: Consumer verification guide
docs.text:
This guide explains how an everyday reader should review a product passport or verification page without needing to understand the underlying technical stack.

Public-facing guide

This guide is written for a future public verification experience. It is still useful now as a content and UX benchmark.

docs.title: Public verification checklist
docs.text:
1. Read the summary first - Make sure the product, producer, and region are easy to understand in plain language.

  2. Look for who verified what - Do not rely on a generic badge alone; check whether the page names the organization behind key claims.

  3. Check the status - A trustworthy page tells you whether the record is verified, incomplete, changed later, or under review.

  4. Open supporting evidence only if needed - If something matters to your decision, confirm that the supporting document or note exists.

  5. Notice what is missing - Absence of evidence is not the same as a failed claim, but it should lower confidence when the claim matters.

docs.title: Copy choices matter
docs.text: Better wording Avoid Verified against the currently committed record Guaranteed authentic forever No matching attestation is currently available Everything is fine This record changed after an earlier commitment Minor update with no explanation Issued by the named organization Certified, without naming by whom

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• how-verification-works - See the technical logic behind this simpler checklist.
  • example-product-passport - See the record shape that this guide assumes.
  • faq - Answer common trust questions after the checklist.

docs.title: Data handling and privacy
docs.text:
Trustworthy provenance systems need to separate what must be public, what can stay private, and what should only be revealed to the right operational role.

Reference data policy

This page documents the privacy boundaries future Terroir implementations should respect. It does not represent a live privacy policy for unshipped services.

docs.title: Privacy principle
docs.text: A trustworthy system is not a system that publishes everything. It is a system that publishes enough for the intended decision while protecting what should remain private, commercially sensitive, or operationally restricted.

docs.title: A practical separation model
docs.text:
Data class Should usually be visible to the public? Examples Public trust signals Yes Origin summary, issuer names, high-level verification status, public certificates. Restricted operational detail Sometimes Internal workflow notes, precise contact details, shipment exceptions, non-public evidence links. Private or sensitive detail No Personally identifying information, secret commercial terms, raw credentials, internal access tokens.

This separation also helps keep provenance useful across different deployment contexts. Open digital public goods patterns encourage reuse, but reuse only works when communities can adapt visibility rules to local needs rather than adopting a one-size-fits-all disclosure model.[1][2]

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• security-and-limitations - Read the security caveats that sit next to the privacy design.
  • data-model - See how private and public data separate within the reference model.
  • consumer-verification-guide - Compare internal data policy with the end-user view.

docs.text:
1. Traceability & recalls (FAO, official). Source. FAO guidance on why traceability matters in food systems.
  2. Digital Public Goods Standard (Digital Public Goods Alliance, standards). Source. DPG requirements and indicators.

docs.title: Data model
docs.text:
The reference data model organizes provenance around producers, batches, attestations, evidence, and verification outputs so that each claim can stay linked to the actor and context that created it.

Reference schema

This is a structured target model for future implementation. The website repository itself does not contain a live provenance database.

docs.title: Reference model shape
docs.text: At the center of the model sits a batch or passport record. Around it are evidence-linked attestations, supporting media or documents, and a verification view that translates the raw record into something a human can assess.

docs.title: Core entities
docs.text: Entity Purpose Key questions it answers Producer Represents the origin actor. Who is making the initial claim and where do they operate? Batch / passport Defines the specific lot or unit being described. What product is this, from where, and for which time window? Attestation Carries a role-specific statement about the batch. Who verified or observed what? Evidence asset Supports the attestation with files or documents. What concrete material backs the claim? Verification view Packages the record for review. What should a buyer or consumer see first?

docs.title: Rules that keep the model trustworthy
docs.text:
• Batch identity should stay stable even when later claims are added.

  • Each attestation should point back to both the batch and the issuer who made the claim.

  • Evidence should be linked by reference so that verification can explain which files support which claims.

  • Integrity commitments should cover the fields whose silent change would undermine trust.

docs.title: Example product passport
docs.text:
A trustworthy product passport should let a reader move from story to evidence without losing track of who said what and what still needs review.

Reference UX artifact

This page describes the intended shape of a readable passport and can guide design work before the verification application exists.

docs.title: Product passport anatomy
docs.text: A readable passport needs both human narrative and structured trust signals. Alt text: A center label product passport surrounded by origin summary, attestation chain, evidence links, status signal, shipment context, and integrity record. Layout: anatomy - Center: Product passport - Origin summary: Where the batch comes from, in plain language. - Attestation chain: Who added which claims and in what role. - Evidence links: Files, reports, photos, or certificates backing those claims. - Status signal: Verified, incomplete, changed, disputed, or superseded. - Shipment context: Optional logistics or commercial handoff context. - Integrity record: The commitment that lets later readers check for silent change.

docs.title: Recommended reading order
docs.text:
1. Start with the origin summary - The first screen should explain the product and why the record exists.

  2. Check the status badge - A reader should immediately see whether the record is verified, incomplete, or changed later.

  3. Open the attestation chain - Details should explain which claims come from which parties.

  4. Inspect evidence only when needed - Evidence should be available without overwhelming the first-time reader.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• consumer-verification-guide - See how a casual reader should use the passport.
  • batch-schema - Review the structured fields behind the passport.
  • how-verification-works - Understand the verification flow the passport must support.

docs.title: Hashing model
docs.text:
The hashing model defines which parts of a record should be committed for later integrity checks and which parts can remain flexible or private.

Reference integrity pattern

The docs describe the intended commitment strategy. The website repository does not yet implement canonical hashing utilities for these records.

docs.title: Why a hashing model exists
docs.text: Hashing is useful when you need later readers to detect silent change. It is not useful when you only want to store data more compactly. In Terroir, the hashing model is a trust device: it defines which fields are important enough that changing them later should trigger a visible difference.

docs.title: Good candidates for commitment
docs.text:
• Stable identifiers that tie later evidence to the same batch or attestation.

  • Core descriptive fields whose change would alter the meaning of the claim.

  • Issuer-linked attestation payloads that need later integrity review.

  • Pointers to evidence sets when those pointers themselves are part of the claim.

What usually should not be hashed directly

Ephemeral UI state, generated summaries, and fields that are expected to change operationally without changing the trust meaning of the underlying claim.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• batch-schema - Review the core batch fields first.
  • verification-logic - See how commitment mismatches should be interpreted.
  • security-and-limitations - Read the limitations of hash-only trust guarantees.

docs.title: How verification works
docs.text:
Verification in Terroir means checking whether a readable product record, its supporting claims, and its integrity commitments still line up with one another.

Reference verification flow

The verification logic described here is the intended reference model. This repository does not yet implement the full verification service or scanner experience.

docs.title: What verification means
docs.text:
Verification is not the same thing as displaying information. A page can show a producer name, a harvest date, and a certification badge without proving any of it. Verification begins when the record tells you who made each claim, what evidence supports it, and whether the critical parts of that record still match their trusted commitment.

Food-system guidance often treats traceability as a chain of accountability, not just a chain of data.[1] Attestation systems make that explicit by tying claims to identifiable issuers and reusable evidence structures.[2]

docs.title: Verification flow
docs.text: A readable verification experience should move from claim to evidence to integrity check without hiding uncertainty. Alt text: A five-step flow showing passport retrieval, claim review, issuer check, integrity comparison, and human decision. Layout: flow - Retrieve passport: Load the human-readable record for the batch or shipment. - Read claims: Identify which statements come from the producer and which come from other actors. - Check issuers: Confirm whether certifiers, labs, or other actors are the expected parties. - Compare integrity record: Verify that the committed record still matches the current one. - Decide with context: Surface whether the record is consistent, incomplete, or changed later.

docs.title: Verification needs graduated outcomes
docs.text: Outcome Meaning What the UI should say Verified The current record matches the stored integrity commitment and issuer expectations. This record is internally consistent and traceable to the referenced claims. Incomplete The record may be readable but is missing expected attestations or issuer context. Some claims are present without the expected supporting evidence. Changed after commitment Important fields no longer match the integrity commitment. The current record differs from the committed version and should be reviewed. Untrusted issuer The claim exists but comes from an actor outside the expected trust model. A claim was made by a party that the workflow does not treat as authoritative for that claim type.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:

  • verification-logic - Continue into the structured logic and failure modes.

docs.title: Security and limitations
docs.text:
Terroir can improve trust, but it cannot make bad evidence true, prevent every fraud tactic, or replace operational governance. This page explains both the safeguards and the limits.

Risk guide

This page captures the risk boundaries future Terroir implementations should acknowledge explicitly rather than hiding behind technical language.

docs.title: Foundational safeguards
docs.text:
• Use least-privilege access rules so actors can only issue or change the claims appropriate to their role.

  • Preserve audit history for important changes instead of overwriting records silently.

  • Protect secrets and operational credentials outside public verification surfaces.

  • Treat verification UI language as part of security because misleading labels create false trust. 1 2

docs.title: What the system cannot do on its own
Trust boundaries
docs.text:
Risk What Terroir can help with What still requires governance or human review False original input It can make later tampering visible. It cannot prove that a false original statement was true at the moment of entry. Weak issuer governance It can record who made a claim. It cannot decide whether a badly chosen issuer should have been trusted in the first place. Poor evidence quality It can link evidence to a claim. It cannot make blurry photos or weak inspection practices reliable. Overclaiming by UI It can show graded verification states. Teams still need to choose careful wording that does not imply certainty where there is only partial evidence.

Trust is layered, not magical

Integrity commitments, attestation protocols, and access control help, but they do not replace field inspection, competent certifiers, or honest operational process.

docs.title: Trust model
docs.text:
The trust model explains who is allowed to say what, who is expected to review it, and how different kinds of claims should gain or lose weight over time.

Reference trust rules

This page describes the governance logic that future Terroir workflows should enforce. It is not yet represented by live authorization services in this repository.

docs.title: The core rule
docs.text: Terroir does not try to make every claim equally trustworthy. It assumes that different actors have authority over different statements. A producer can describe their own batch. A certifier can attest to what they inspected. A lab can report a test result. A buyer can confirm receipt. The system becomes more trustworthy when those roles are clear and challengeable.

docs.title: Who should say what
docs.text:
Actor Good claim types Claims that should stay outside their authority Producer Batch identity, origin narrative, production context, declared process. Self-issued regulatory approval or independent quality certification. Certifier or regulator Inspection outcome, standards compliance, protected-origin review. Changing producer-supplied batch facts without an explicit amendment trail. Lab Test results linked to method and sample. Commercial origin guarantees without chain context. Logistics partner Shipment custody, handoff, route events. Agronomic or quality claims they did not observe. Buyer Receipt, acceptance, or discrepancy reports. Retroactive certification of upstream conditions.

This aligns with role-oriented authorization patterns used in many contract and application designs: authority is limited by purpose, not just by whether a user is logged in.[1][2]

docs.title: Implications for future product design
docs.text:
• Treat issuer identity as part of the claim, not as surrounding metadata that can be ignored.

  • Show the difference between producer-supplied facts and independently attested claims.

  • Preserve amendments instead of silently overwriting significant fields.

  • Favor explicit trust states such as verified, pending, disputed, or superseded.

docs.title: Verification logic
docs.text:
Verification logic turns the trust model into a repeatable decision process by checking identity, claim scope, evidence presence, and integrity consistency.

Reference verification rules

This logic is documented as a target behavior for future services and verification views.

docs.title: Reference logic
docs.text:
1. Confirm record identity - The verifier should know exactly which batch or passport is under review.

  2. Check issuer fitness - Each attestation must come from an actor appropriate to that claim type.

  3. Check evidence completeness - Important claims should point to supporting records or explain why evidence is absent.

  4. Check integrity consistency - Committed fields should still match the current record.

  5. Return a graded result - The outcome should distinguish verified, incomplete, changed, and disputed states.

docs.title: Failures a good verifier should name explicitly
docs.text:
• The batch exists but lacks the attestation type needed for the current decision.

  • An attestation is present but the issuer is not the right authority for that claim.

  • The evidence link resolves, but the record has changed since the commitment was made.

  • The record is internally consistent but still incomplete for the buyer's risk threshold.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• how-verification-works - Start with the plain-language explanation first.
  • trust-model - See which actors are expected to make which claims.
  • consumer-verification-guide - Translate these rules into a human checklist.

docs.title: Buyer and importer guide
docs.text:
This guide focuses on the buyer decision: what a commercial reviewer needs to see before they can rely on a provenance record during sourcing or import review.

Reference commercial workflow

The guide documents the intended verification flow for commercial users before the underlying operational tooling exists in this repo.

docs.title: Buyer goal
docs.text: A buyer or importer does not need every operational detail. They need enough trustworthy information to answer a business question: should this batch be accepted, escalated for review, or rejected because the trust story is incomplete or inconsistent?

docs.title: Commercial verification questions
docs.text: Question What the buyer should look for Why it matters Is this the right product and lot? Stable batch identifier, product summary, and origin context. Prevents confusion between adjacent lots or marketing narratives. Who made the important claims? Named certifiers, labs, or operational actors rather than anonymous badges. Makes accountability visible. Is the verification state clear? A graded status rather than a generic green check. Lets the buyer match trust depth to commercial risk. What remains unresolved? Explicit missing evidence or open issues. Allows conditional decisions instead of false certainty.

docs.title: Expected outputs
docs.text:
• A readable product passport or export-ready verification view.

  • Access to the attestation chain for claims that affect the purchase decision.

  • A visible explanation of any incomplete, changed, or disputed state.

  • A stable link or QR destination so later audits point back to the same record.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• how-verification-works - Review the underlying verification logic.
  • exporter-workflows - See what the exporter side should package for commercial review.
  • consumer-verification-guide - Compare the commercial checklist with the public one.

docs.title: Certifier onboarding
docs.text:
Certifier onboarding is where the trust model becomes operational: a future implementation will need a careful process for deciding who can issue which attestation types and under what conditions.

Reference issuer workflow

This page documents the intended onboarding expectations for certifiers before role management and attestation tooling are implemented.

docs.title: Why certifier onboarding must be careful
docs.text:
A trust system fails quickly if anyone can issue authoritative claims without review. Terroir therefore treats certifier onboarding as a governance step, not a self-service growth funnel.

Practical reading

The current website does not yet implement a certifier registry or approval flow. This page exists to document the control points that future work should preserve.

docs.title: Issuer onboarding steps
docs.text:
1. Confirm organizational identity - Document the institution behind the certifier account.

  2. Define attestation scope - List which claim types the certifier is actually qualified to issue.

  3. Set review and renewal rules - Decide how status is rechecked over time and who can suspend authority.

  4. Publish readable issuer metadata - Future verification views should make the certifier understandable to downstream readers.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• trust-model - Review the role matrix behind onboarding.
  • attestation-schema - See the structure of the claims a certifier would issue.
  • security-and-limitations - Understand why certifier governance matters as much as the technology.

docs.title: Exporter workflows
docs.text:
Exporter workflows sit between producer records and commercial verification, packaging the right provenance story for cross-border review without forcing every reviewer into the same system.

Reference export workflow

This page describes the intended exporter-facing layer and does not correspond to a live export module in the current codebase.

docs.title: Exporter purpose
docs.text: Exporters need to turn a potentially complex origin record into a format another organization can review quickly. That means summarizing the trust story without hiding the evidence path behind it.

docs.title: Exporter path
docs.text:
1. Select the shipment-linked batches - Identify which batch records are part of the exported lot.

  2. Assemble the verification package - Combine origin summaries, issuer context, and verification status in a readable export view.

  3. Expose stable verification access - Share a link, QR destination, or document package that can be revisited later.

  4. Record downstream review outcomes - Track whether the buyer accepted, questioned, or requested more evidence.

docs.title: Minimum export package
docs.text:
• A clear shipment-to-batch mapping.

  • Named attestations that matter to the trade decision.

  • Any unresolved limitations or missing evidence called out plainly.

  • Stable references for later customs, distributor, or buyer review.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• buyer-and-importer-guide - See how the packaged output should be consumed.
  • consumer-verification-guide - Compare commercial and public verification needs.
  • data-handling-and-privacy - Review which details should stay restricted.

docs.title: Integration guides
docs.text:
Terroir is meant to fit around existing agricultural operations, not replace every system around it. This page explains the main integration patterns the future stack should support.

Reference integration patterns

This repository documents integration patterns but does not yet ship the corresponding operational APIs or widgets.

docs.title: Integration modes
docs.text: Pattern What it connects Why it matters Operational data sync Producer systems and future batch-management services. Reduces duplicate entry. Issuer workflow integration Certifier or lab tools and attestation issuance flows. Lets independent actors stay in their own systems where possible. Verification embedding Retail, export, or partner-facing interfaces and public verification views. Moves trust evidence closer to the decision point. Registry or customs handoff Country- or sector-level systems and product passport summaries. Supports broader interoperability without forcing a single monolith.

docs.title: Guidance for future adapters
docs.text:
• Keep the record portable so communities are not trapped in one vendor or deployment model.

  • Separate public verification outputs from private operational credentials.

  • Design for gradual adoption: a workflow should still work if only part of the chain has integrated.

  • Use stable identifiers and explicit versioning when schemas evolve.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:

  • api-verification - See the public verification interface concept.
  • app-frontend - Understand how future UX surfaces could consume shared data.

docs.title: Producer journey
docs.text:
The producer journey explains how a future Terroir workflow should move from batch creation to outside verification without forcing the producer to become a technical expert.

Reference workflow

This is a future-facing workflow guide. The current repository does not yet implement a live producer dashboard or batch-management application.

docs.title: Producer goal
docs.text: A producer should be able to describe a batch once, attach the right evidence over time, and share a verification story without needing to master the underlying infrastructure. The product experience should reduce paperwork friction, not replace one confusing process with another.

docs.title: Producer path
docs.text:
1. Create the batch record - Define the product lot, origin context, and initial narrative in a stable passport.

  2. Attach supporting evidence - Link the operational records that support the origin story or later certification needs.

  3. Invite or coordinate attestations - Share the batch with certifiers, labs, cooperatives, or other trusted actors.

  4. Review verification readiness - See whether the batch is complete enough for export, compliance, or consumer presentation.

  5. Publish or share the verification view - Create the readable output used by buyers, importers, or consumers.

docs.title: What producers need from the product
Non-negotiables
docs.text:
• Clear separation between draft work and externally visible trust signals.

  • The ability to reuse data across export, certification, and storytelling workflows.

  • Simple language explaining which missing items actually block verification.

  • Visible history when important fields change or claims are superseded.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:

  • certifier-onboarding - See how external actors join the producer's trust chain.
  • example-product-passport - See the expected public-facing outcome.

ამ განსხვავების ხაზგასმა აუცილებელია, რათა დოკუმენტაციამ ნდობა გაზარდოს და არა პირიქით. კარგი არქიტექტურული დოკუმენტი ერთდროულად უნდა აჩვენებდეს ხედვასაც და მიმდინარე რეალობასაც.

docs.text:

კონცეპტუალური მოდელი

ეს ტერმინები reference მოდელს აღწერს და ჯერ არაა წარმოდგენილი სრული აპლიკაციის სახით ამ რეპოზიტორიაში.

docs.title: ძირითადი ობიექტები
docs.text:
Terroir ოთხ ძირითად პრიმიტივზეა აგებული: Producers, Batches, Attestations და Verification Proofs. Producer არის აქტორი, რომელიც წარმოშობის საწყის მტკიცებას აკეთებს; Batch არის ის კონკრეტული პროდუქტის ერთეული, რომლის შესახებაც ვსაუბრობთ.

Attestation წარმოადგენს ხელმოწერილ მტკიცებას კონკრეტულ პარტიაზე: მას შეიძლება აკეთებდეს სერტიფიკატორი, ლაბორატორია, კოოპერატივი ან სხვა ავტორიზებული მხარე. Verification view არის ის ადამიანისთვის წაკითხვადი ფორმა, სადაც ეს ყველაფერი ერთ სურათად იყრის თავს.

docs.title: ამ რეპოზიტორიის დაწყება
docs.text:
თუ თქვენი მიზანია საიტზე და დოკუმენტაციაზე მუშაობა, რეალური setup მარტივია: root დირექტორიაში dependencies-ის დაყენება, npm run dev-ით საიტის გაშვება და npm run lint-ით ვალიდაცია.

Quickstart-ის სხვა ნაწილი აღწერს უფრო დიდ provenance stack-ს — მონაცემთა ფენას, attestations-სა და verification სერვისებს — მაგრამ ეს ყველაფერი ჯერ reference მიმართულებაა და ამ კოდბაზაში განხორციელებული არ არის.

docs.text:

  • app-frontend - გაიგეთ სად მთავრდება დღევანდელი frontend.

docs.title: რა არის Terroir
docs.text:
Terroir არის ღია ინფრასტრუქტურის კონცეფცია და დოკუმენტაციის სივრცე სანდო აგრარული წარმომავლობისთვის.

პროექტის მიმოხილვა

ამ რეპოზიტორიაში დღეს ძირითადად საიტი და დოკუმენტაციაა; ფართო პლატფორმა დოკუმენტებში reference მოდელადაა აღწერილი.

docs.title: მოკლედ
docs.text:
Terroir არის ღია კოდის პროტოკოლი და პლატფორმის კონცეფცია აგრარული provenance-ისთვის. მისი მიზანია მცირე მწარმოებლებს, კოოპერატივებსა და სერტიფიკატორებს ჰქონდეთ უფრო სანდო გზა წარმოშობის, პროცესისა და ხარისხის დასადასტურებლად.

პროექტში თითოეული პარტია პირველ კლასის ციფრულ ობიექტად მოიაზრება: ვინ აწარმოა, სად, როდის, როგორ დამუშავდა და ვინ რა დაადასტურა. კრიტიკული მტკიცებულება უნდა იყოს tamper-evident, ხოლო ადამიანისთვის წაკითხვადი ნაწილი — მარტივი და გამოსადეგი.

დღევანდელ რეპოზიტორიაში წარმოდგენილია საიტი, კვლევის გვერდები, თეთრი ნაშრომი და დოკუმენტაციის ბიბლიოთეკა. უფრო ფართო ოპერაციული პლატფორმა ამ ეტაპზე დოკუმენტური reference მიმართულებაა.

docs.title: ატესტაციის ენდპოინტები
docs.text:
Attestation endpoints უნდა ეხმარებოდეს issuer-ებს role-scoped claim-ების უსაფრთხოდ გაცემაში.

reference issuer endpoint

ამ ეტაპზე attestation API ამ რეპოზიტორიაში არ არის.

docs.text: Attestation endpoint-მა უნდა დაადასტუროს, რომ actor-ს აქვს შესაბამისი scope, claim-ს აქვს სტრუქტურული ფორმა და evidence-თან კავშირი, ხოლო ცვლილებები ისტორიას ინარჩუნებს.

docs.title: API ავტენტიკაცია
docs.text:
სამომავლო API-სთვის მთავარი ზღვარი public verification-სა და privileged write ოპერაციებს შორის გადის.

reference API boundary

live API route-ები ამ რეპოზიტორიაში არ არის; გვერდი მომავალი trust boundary-ს აღწერს.

docs.title: რა უნდა განვასხვავოთ
docs.text: Public verification ideally უნდა რჩებოდეს ადვილად ხელმისაწვდომი, ხოლო write ოპერაციები მკაცრად უნდა იყოს მიბმული actor identity-სა და role scope-ზე.

docs.title: Related Pages

  • api-attestation - იხილეთ issuer-side endpoint.
  • api-verification - იხილეთ public read interface.

docs.title: ბეჩის შექმნის API
docs.text:
Batch creation API უნდა ქმნიდეს სტაბილურ პარტიის იდენტობას და არა უბრალოდ arbitrary field dump-ს.

reference producer endpoint

batch-ების შექმნის live endpoint ამ რეპოზიტორიაში არ არსებობს.

docs.title: რისთვის არის endpoint
docs.text: Batch creation endpoint-ის მთავარი ამოცანაა producer input-იდან სტაბილური passport-ს მსგავსი ჩანაწერის შექმნა, draft/public/committed მდგომარეობების არევა კი არა.

docs.title: საჯარო ვერიფიკაციის API
docs.text:
Public verification API უნდა აბრუნებდეს მკაფიო, graded შედეგებს ისე, რომ გაურკვევლობა არ დაიმალოს.

reference public endpoint

public verification route ამ რეპოზიტორიაში ჯერ არ არის.

docs.text: Verification API-ს უნდა შეეძლოს readable summary-ის, attestation chain-ისა და integrity comparison-ის დაბრუნება ისე, რომ პასუხში გამოირჩეს verified, incomplete, changed ან disputed მდგომარეობები.

docs.title: გარემოს ცვლადები
docs.text:
Environment variable surface მომავალში მკაფიოდ უნდა ჰყოფდეს public config-სა და server-only secret-ებს.

reference configuration surface

ამ რეპოზიტორიაში ფართო backend config არ არსებობს; გვერდი სამომავლო წესებს აღწერს.

docs.title: რატომ არის config boundary მნიშვნელოვანი
docs.text: Configuration-მა უნდა თქვას რომელი პარამეტრებია უსაფრთხო client exposure-ისთვის და რომელი secrets არასდროს უნდა გავიდეს browser-ში. ეს trust boundary-ს ნაწილია.

docs.title: ფრონტენდის არქიტექტურა
docs.text:
დღეს repo-ის frontend არის public website და docs; ოპერაციული frontend მომავალ reference layer-ად უნდა წავიკითხოთ.

producer/issuer/public operational frontend ამ რეპოზიტორიაში ჯერ არ არის; დღეს გვაქვს docs და marketing experience.

docs.text: მომავალ frontend-ს უნდა შეეძლოს producers, issuers და public verifiers სხვადასხვა trust state-ების მკაფიოდ დანახვა. დღევანდელი frontend კი public site-სა და documentation layer-ს მოიცავს.

docs.text:
Reference app-ის გვერდი დღეს უნდა წავიკითხოთ როგორც მომავალი ოპერაციული აპლიკაციის კონტური.

reference app concept

producer/certifier reference app ამ რეპოზიტორიაში ჯერ არ არსებობს.

docs.text: Reference app უნდა იყოს ოპერაციული ფენა, სადაც producers ქმნიან ჩანაწერებს, issuers გასცემენ claim-ებს და იქმნება verification view. ამჟამინდელი რეპოზიტორია კი public website-ს ფარავს.

reference data layer

docs.title: როგორ უნდა წავიკითხოთ ეს გვერდი
docs.text: Reference data layer-ს უნდა შეეძლოს producers, batches, attestations და verification outputs-ის გამოყოფა ისე, რომ history, evidence linkage და public/private split არ დაიკარგოს. თუმცა რეალური migrations ამ კოდბაზაში არ არის.

docs.text:
Event-ები მომავალი integrity ფენის audit trail-ს შექმნისთვისაა საჭირო.

reference event surface

ამ რეპოზიტორიაში event-producing contract არ არსებობს.

docs.title: რატომ გვჭირდება ივენთები
docs.text: Event-ები საჭიროა იმისთვის, რომ integrity-სთან დაკავშირებული ცვლილებები downstream სისტემებმა სწრაფად და ერთმნიშვნელოვნად დაინახონ: რა დაემატა, რა შეიცვალა authority-ში, რა superseded ან disputed გახდა.

docs.title: Related Pages

  • function-reference - ნახეთ reference operations.

docs.title: დეპლოის გზამკვლევი
docs.text:
Deployment guide აღწერს იმ გადაწყვეტილებებს, რომლებიც provenance სისტემაში code push-ზე მეტია.

reference deployment guide

დღეს deployable provenance stack ამ რეპოზიტორიაში არ არსებობს; გვერდი მომავალი rollout-ის პრინციპებს აღწერს.

docs.title: რატომ არის deployment განსაკუთრებული
docs.text: ნდობის ინფრასტრუქტურაში deployment მხოლოდ კოდის გაშვება არ არის. მას ახლავს issuer governance, verification continuity, incident response და გარემოების მკაფიო გამიჯვნა demo/pilot/production შორის.

docs.text:

  • governance-roadmap - შეადარეთ მმართველობით გზას.
  • app-setup - იხილეთ app boundary.

docs.title: ფუნქციების რეფერენსი
docs.text:
Reference functions აღწერს მომავალი integrity ინტერფეისის ძირითად read/write ოპერაციებს.

reference interface

ეს არის მხოლოდ აღწერითი ინტერფეისი; live ABI ან service contract ამ რეპოზიტორიაში არ არის.

docs.title: რა ოპერაციები გვჭირდება
docs.text: მომავალ integrity ფენას, დიდი ალბათობით, დასჭირდება რამდენიმე ძირითადი ოპერაცია: record-ის commitment, commitment-ის კითხვა, issuer authority-ის შემოწმება და superseded/disputed მდგომარეობების ხილული დაფიქსირება.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• registry-contract - იხილეთ ამ ფუნქციების კონტექსტი.
  • verification-logic - ნახეთ read paths-ის გამოყენება.
  • deployment-guide - იხილეთ rollout მხარე.

docs.title: რეგისტრის კონტრაქტი
docs.text:
Registry contract გვერდი აღწერს მომავალი integrity ფენის ვიწრო პასუხისმგებლობას.

reference contract design

ამ რეპოზიტორიაში contract-ების პაკეტი ან ABI არ არსებობს; გვერდი სამომავლო როლს აღწერს.

docs.title: რისთვის უნდა არსებობდეს registry
docs.text:
თუ მომავალში Terroir გამოიყენებს on-chain ან სხვა tamper-evident registry-ს, მისი მიზანი უნდა იყოს commitments-ისა და role rules-ის შენახვა, და არა მთელი აპლიკაციის ლოგიკის მასში გადატანა.

ამ კოდბაზაში ასეთი კონტრაქტი ჯერ არ არსებობს, ამიტომ ეს გვერდი design constraint-ად უნდა წაიკითხოთ.

  • function-reference - იხილეთ reference ოპერაციები.
  • deployment-guide - ნახეთ rollout კონტექსტი.

docs.title: ატესტაციის სქემა
docs.text:
Attestation schema აღწერს ვინ ამბობს რას, რომელ batch-ზე და რა evidence-ით.

reference claim model

ეს სქემა მომავალ claim-ების მოდელს აღწერს და არა მიმდინარე სერვისს.

docs.title: ატესტაციის ფორმა
docs.text:
Attestation უნდა ინახავდეს batch-ის ბმას, issuer-ის იდენტობას, claim-ის ტიპს, evidence-ის კვალს და claim-ის მდგომარეობას. ამგვარად verification-ს შემდეგ შეუძლია არა მხოლოდ ტექსტის წაკითხვა, არამედ იმის გარჩევაც, ვინ თქვა რა და რაზე დაყრდნობით.

ქვეტიპები შეიძლება განსხვავდებოდეს: origin review, quality claim, lab result ან სხვა. თუმცა საერთო rule უცვლელია — claim უნდა იყოს issuer-თან და context-თან მჭიდროდ შეკრული.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• verification-logic - იხილეთ როგორ მოწმდება claim-ების თანხვედრა.
  • certifier-onboarding - ნახეთ issuer-ების ოპერაციული მხარე.
  • trust-model - ინგლისურად წაიკითხეთ role matrix.

docs.title: ბეჩის სქემა
docs.text:
Batch schema განსაზღვრავს იმ მინიმალურ ინფორმაციას, რომლის გარეშეც წარმოშობის მტკიცება ბუნდოვანი რჩება.

reference batch shape

ეს გვერდი აღწერს მიზნობრივ პარტიის ფორმას და არა live object-ს.

docs.title: რას უნდა შეიცავდეს პარტია
docs.text:
პარტიის ჩანაწერმა უნდა აღწეროს კონკრეტული ერთეული: რა პროდუქტია, ვინ აწარმოა, სად არის მისი origin კონტექსტი და რა დროის ან პროცესის ჭრილს ეკუთვნის. მიზანი ყველაფრის ერთ ობიექტში მოთავსება არაა; მიზანია ძირითადი იდენტობა და წარმოშობის ჩარჩო მკაფიო იყოს.

უმჯობესია core schema იყოს მცირე და სტაბილური, ხოლო richer context ატესტაციებში, evidence-ში ან commodity-specific გაფართოებებში გადაინაცვლოს.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• hashing-model - იხილეთ რომელი ველები უნდა იყოს integrity commitment-ში.
  • producer-journey - ინგლისურად ნახეთ batch-ის workflow.
  • example-product-passport - ინგლისურად ნახეთ მომხმარებლისთვის წაკითხვადი შედეგი.

docs.title: მონაცემთა მოდელი
docs.text:
მონაცემთა მოდელი producers, batches და attestations ობიექტების გარშემოა აგებული.

reference სქემა

ეს აღწერს მიზნობრივ მონაცემთა მოდელს და არა უკვე არსებულ მონაცემთა ბაზას ამ რეპოზიტორიაში.

docs.title: მოდელის ლოგიკა
docs.text:
Terroir-ის reference მონაცემთა მოდელი ისეთ მინიმალურ ერთეულებზეა აგებული, რომლებიც provenance-ისთვის საკმარისია და თან სხვადასხვა პროდუქტის შემთხვევაშიც გამოსადეგია. ცენტრში დგას პარტია, მის გარშემო კი მწარმოებელი, attestations და evidence-თან დაკავშირებული ობიექტები.

მთავარი პრინციპი ის არის, რომ claim უნდა დარჩეს დაკავშირებული როგორც batch-თან, ისე იმ აქტორთან, ვინც ეს claim გააკეთა. ამით verification-ის დროს ჩანაწერის მნიშვნელობა და პასუხისმგებლობა არ იკარგება.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• batch-schema - იხილეთ პარტიის ძირითადი ველები.
  • attestation-schema - იხილეთ claim-ების სტრუქტურა.
  • data-handling-and-privacy - ინგლისურ ვერსიაში წაიკითხეთ public/private გაყოფა.

docs.title: ჰეშირების მოდელი
docs.text:
ჰეშირების მოდელი წყვეტს რომელი ველები უნდა იყოს ისეთი მნიშვნელოვანი, რომ მათი შემდგომი ცვლილება მაშინვე შესამჩნევი გახდეს.

reference integrity pattern

ეს აღწერს commitment სტრატეგიას და არა უკვე არსებულ util-ებს ამ კოდბაზაში.

docs.title: რატომ გვჭირდება hashing მოდელი
docs.text:
ჰეში აქ უბრალოდ compact storage არაა; ის trust მექანიზმია. იდეაა, რომ ყველაზე მნიშვნელოვანი ველები ისეთი ფორმით იყოს შეკრული, რომ მათი შემდგომი უხმო ცვლილება verification-ისას გამოაშკარავდეს.

ამიტომ hashing მოდელი უნდა იყოს ფრთხილი: უნდა მოიცავდეს იმ ნაწილს, რომლის შეცვლაც claim-ის მნიშვნელობას ცვლის, და არ უნდა ახშობდეს ისეთ ოპერაციულ დეტალებს, რომლებიც ბუნებრივად იცვლება.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• verification-logic - ნახეთ როგორ იკითხება mismatch-ები.
  • batch-schema - იხილეთ core ველები.
  • security-and-limitations - ინგლისურად წაიკითხეთ hash-only მიდგომის ზღვარი.

docs.title: ვერიფიკაციის ლოგიკა
docs.text:
Verification logic არის განმეორებადი წესი, რომლითაც მოწმდება იდენტობა, issuer-ი, evidence და integrity თანხვედრა.

reference verification rules

ეს წესები სამომავლო სერვისების სამიზნე ქცევას აღწერს.

docs.title: როგორ უნდა წავიკითხოთ verification
docs.text:
ვერიფიკაცია იწყება იმით, რომ მკაფიოა რომელ batch-ზეა საუბარი. შემდეგ მოწმდება ვინ გააკეთა claim, ეკუთვნის თუ არა ეს claim მის authority-ს, არსებობს თუ არა შესაბამისი evidence და ემთხვევა თუ არა ჩანაწერი ადრე დაფიქსირებულ integrity commitment-ს.

კარგმა verification შედეგმა მხოლოდ pass/fail არ უნდა თქვას. მას უნდა შეეძლოს თქვას: verified, incomplete, changed later ან disputed — რათა მომხმარებელმა ზუსტად გაიგოს რა ხარისხის ნდობასთან აქვს საქმე.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• api-verification - იხილეთ public verification interface-ის reference აღწერა.
  • consumer-verification-guide - ინგლისურად ნახეთ ადვილად წასაკითხი checklist.
  • trust-model - ინგლისურად ნახეთ authority boundaries.

docs.title: სერტიფიკატორის ონბორდინგი
docs.text:
Certifier onboarding არის governance ნაბიჯი, სადაც იკვეთება ვინ რა ტიპის claim-ს გასცემს.

reference issuer workflow

სერტიფიკატორის registry და approval flow ამ ეტაპზე ჯერ არ არის განხორციელებული.

docs.title: რატომ არის ეს ფრთხილი პროცესი
docs.text:
თუ authority ყველასთვის ღია და გაუმჭვირვალეა, provenance სისტემის ნდობა სწრაფად ინგრევა. ამიტომ certifier onboarding უნდა იყოს კონტროლირებული governance პროცესი და არა უბრალო თვითრეგისტრაცია.

პრაქტიკულად ეს ნიშნავს ორგანიზაციული იდენტობის დადასტურებას, claim scope-ის განსაზღვრას და review/renewal წესების დოკუმენტირებას.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• attestation-schema - იხილეთ claim-ების სტრუქტურა.
  • verification-logic - ნახეთ როგორ მოწმდება ეს claim-ები.
  • governance-roadmap - შეადარეთ მმართველობით მოდელს.

docs.title: ექსპორტერის სამუშაო ნაკადები
docs.text:
ექსპორტერის workflow producer-ის ჩანაწერს ამზადებს ისე, რომ buyer-მა ან customs-მა მისი სწრაფი გადამოწმება შეძლოს.

reference export flow

ეს აღწერა export მოდულის მიზნობრივ სურათს ასახავს და არა live ფუნქციონალს.

docs.title: workflow-ის ამოცანა
docs.text:
ექსპორტერის ამოცანაა შეაგროვოს სწორი batch-ები, შეკრას verification package და მეორე მხარეს გადასცეს ისეთი ფორმით, რომ ჩანაწერი მოგვიანებითაც ხელახლა შემოწმებადი დარჩეს.

ეს package უნდა შეიცავდეს origin summary-ს, issuer context-ს, verification status-ს და სტაბილურ ბმას იმ provenance ჩანაწერთან, რომელსაც buyer ან regulator მოგვიანებით დაუბრუნდება.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• integrations - იხილეთ broader integration patterns.
  • verification-logic - ნახეთ verification-ის შინაგანი წესი.
  • buyer-and-importer-guide - ინგლისურად წაიკითხეთ buyer-ის მხარე.

docs.title: ინტეგრაციის გზამკვლევები
docs.text:
Terroir-ის იდეა არსებულ სამუშაო პროცესებთან დაკავშირებაა და არა ყველა სისტემის ერთდროული ჩანაცვლება.

reference integration patterns

ინტეგრაციის ეს აღწერა სამომავლოა; შესაბამისი API-ები და სერვისები ამ რეპოზიტორიაში არ არის.

docs.title: ინტეგრაციის ძირითადი იდეები
docs.text:
სამომავლო ინტეგრაციები შეიძლება ეხებოდეს producer-სისტემების data sync-ს, issuer workflow-ების დაკავშირებას ან verification view-ის ჩაშენებას სხვა public თუ trade ინტერფეისებში.

მთავარი მიზანია provenance ჩანაწერი პორტაბელური იყოს და adoption ეტაპობრივად მოხდეს, ისე რომ მთელი ჯაჭვის ერთდროული გადაყვანა აუცილებელი არ გახდეს.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• api-authentication - იხილეთ სამომავლო API boundary.
  • api-verification - იხილეთ public verification interface-ის ჩანაფიქრი.
  • architecture - შეადარეთ სისტემურ სურათს.

docs.title: Research Library
docs.text: Evidence sources that support Terroir whitepaper claims and architecture decisions.

docs.title: Record revenues from wine and spirits exports
docs.text:
• Type: official
  • Year: 2025

docs.title: Counterfeit wine labeled with appellation of origin 'Kindzmarauli' revealed in Russia
docs.text:

docs.title: Counterfeit Georgian wine batch removed from sale in Ukraine

docs.title: Index insurance for agricultural resilience: Promises, challenges and solutions
docs.text:
• Type: report
